title: Agentic Research Platform — System Design
description: Design the platform for scientific-research agents — literature review, hypothesis generation, experimental design, and running experiments through lab automation — where the high-stakes action is a physical experiment that consumes reagents, drives a lab robot, or carries biosafety implications. It adds a gate the rest of the series doesn't have: a wet-lab boundary. The agent proposes; a human authorizes anything physical. It builds on Agentic Platform Foundations and adds the things that make research distinct: grounding in primary literature with provenance for every claim, a deterministic protocol validator with dual-use/biosafety screening the model cannot override, reproducibility as a first-class property, and human authorization before any physical run.

nodes.label: Principal Researcher
nodes.description: Scientist who reviews hypotheses and authorizes any physical experiment; the accountable decision-maker.

nodes.label: Biosafety Officer
nodes.description: Authorizes protocols with biosafety / dual-use implications before any wet-lab run.

nodes.label: Research Console
nodes.description: Notebook / API client where a researcher submits a goal (hypothesis, literature question, protocol) and reviews proposals.

nodes.label: Research API
nodes.description: Authenticates the request, resolves lab/tenant scope, routes to the pipeline.

nodes.label: Research Pipeline
nodes.description: Deterministic DAG: retrieve -> hypothesize -> design protocol -> validate -> authorize -> run.

nodes.label: Hypothesis / Design Agent
nodes.description: LLM in a bounded node: reviews literature, forms hypotheses, and drafts an experimental protocol with citations.

nodes.label: Inference Backend
nodes.description: Shared LLM serving tier (from the Foundations platform).

nodes.label: Literature Index
nodes.description: Grounding corpus: primary scientific literature and prior experimental data.

nodes.label: Experimental Data Store
nodes.description: Raw and processed results, versioned, with the run that produced each datum.

nodes.label: Protocol Validator
nodes.description: Deterministic checks: protocol well-formedness, resource limits, and dual-use/biosafety rules the model cannot override.

nodes.label: Experiment Authorization Gate
nodes.description: Routes any physical action (reagents, lab robot, biosafety) to human authorization; dry-run vs wet-lab tier.

nodes.label: Execution Controller
nodes.description: Durable scheduler for physical runs: reserves instruments/reagents, submits jobs with an idempotency key, holds run leases/checkpoints, processes robot callbacks, and supports abort. The only component allowed to submit lab jobs.

nodes.label: Simulation / Analysis Backend
nodes.description: Runs dry-run simulations and analysis — free for the agent, never actuates the physical world.

nodes.label: Lab Automation
nodes.description: Robotic instruments and reagent handling — the physical actuation surface. Rejects any job lacking a valid authorization/run token.

nodes.label: Provenance Store
nodes.description: Maps every claim/finding to its source literature and to the run that produced its data (reproducibility).

nodes.label: Policy & Safety
nodes.description: Tenant isolation, data governance, and screening of untrusted document/data input.

nodes.label: Identity & Token Broker
nodes.description: Scoped, delegated access; binds a wet-lab authorization to a researcher / biosafety principal.

nodes.label: Audit / Lab Notebook
nodes.description: Append-only record: sources, hypotheses, protocols, validations, authorizations, runs, results.

nodes.label: Observability & Eval
nodes.description: Trajectories, reproducibility and claim-grounding metrics, eval vs replicated results.

capacityDiagram: Store[Result + provenance storage grows for years]

functional: Review primary literature and prior data to inform hypotheses.
Generate hypotheses and draft experimental protocols, with citations.
Validate a protocol deterministically before any physical action.
Require human authorization for any experiment that consumes reagents, drives a lab robot, or has biosafety implications.
Run authorized protocols through lab automation and capture results.
nonFunctional: Reproducibility is first-class: every claim and finding traces to its source literature and the run that produced its data.
A deterministic protocol validator (resource limits, dual-use/biosafety rules) gates physical actions; the model cannot override it.
No physical experiment runs without explicit human authorization (dry-run vs wet-lab tiering).
Physical actuation is exactly-once: a retry after an ambiguous timeout must never double-run an experiment.
Dual-use / biosafety hazards are blocked before authorization.
Auditable: a complete lab-notebook record of sources, hypotheses, protocols, authorizations, runs, and results.

capacity.label: Literature corpus
capacity.note: Primary papers, preprints, and prior datasets; re-indexed incrementally with retraction/quality signals.

capacity.label: Retrieval QPS
capacity.note: Grounding lookups during hypothesis and protocol drafting; read-heavy, cacheable per corpus version.

capacity.label: Protocol proposals
capacity.note: Each proposal is an LLM-heavy design pass (multiple reasoning + retrieval calls).

capacity.label: Validations
capacity.note: Deterministic checks are cheap relative to LLM work; throughput is rarely the limit here.

capacity.label: Dry-run simulations
capacity.note: Free for the agent; bounded by simulation/analysis backend compute, not by authorization.

capacity.label: Authorized wet-lab runs
capacity.note: Gated by human authorization AND scarce instrument slots — the real throughput ceiling.

capacity.label: Lab robot concurrency
capacity.note: Physical instruments are the scarcest resource; runs queue against reservations, not CPU.

capacity.label: Run duration
capacity.note: Long, sometimes ambiguous physical actions — drives durable execution, leases, and callbacks.

capacity.label: Result data / run
capacity.note: Instrument output (images, reads, traces); raw artifacts in object storage, metadata in the data store.

capacity.label: Audit events / run
capacity.note: Every source, protocol version, validation, authorization, lease, callback, and result is appended.

capacity.label: Retention
capacity.note: Lab-notebook and provenance records are kept for reproducibility and compliance; storage grows monotonically.

api.description: Propose an experimental protocol from a hypothesis; it is validated and queued for authorization — it does not run.

A researcher (and biosafety officer if required) authorizes a specific protocol version; produces a scoped, signed authorization object. Idempotent — replaying the key returns the same authorization, never a second one.

Start a physical run from an authorization object. Internal: only the execution controller calls this when an authorization is accepted. Idempotent on the key — a retry after an ambiguous timeout resolves to the same run, never a second actuation. Rejected if the authorization is expired, revoked, or its scope doesn't cover the requested instruments/reagents.

messages.label: mint short-lived run token (scoped to version-hash)

api.description: Robot callback ingestion: lab automation reports status, partial progress, or a result for a run. The payload is signed and carries the run token; the controller verifies the signature, rejects replays via a callback sequence number, and treats duplicate callbacks idempotently (last-writer-by-sequence wins).
Operator- or policy-initiated stop of an in-flight run. Sends a stop command to the robot, marks the run aborted (terminal) with a reason, and releases the reservation. Never silently retried.

Resolve an ambiguous run (expired lease, missing callback, unknown state) by querying the robot for the idempotency key's status instead of resubmitting. Returns the reconciled state; 'unknown' stays unknown and is escalated, never blindly retried.

Revoke an authorization. Semantics depend on run state: before scheduling, the run never starts; during reservation, the held slot is released; after submission, revocation triggers an abort of the in-flight run. Revocation is recorded in the audit log and is idempotent.
Fetch the provenance of a finding: source literature for each claim and the run that produced each datum.

dataModel.name: protocols
dataModel.note: A protocol's logical identity and readiness state; the body lives in immutable versions. This table owns protocol/authorization readiness only — physical execution state lives in run_attempts. latest_run_status is a denormalized convenience, derived from run_attempts.

fields.name: readiness
fields.type: enum(proposed,validated,blocked,awaiting_authorization,authorized,rejected) — protocol/authorization readiness only, NOT run state

fields.name: latest_run_status
fields.type: enum(...) null — DERIVED from run_attempts; denormalized for listing, not the source of truth

dataModel.name: protocol_versions
dataModel.note: Immutable, content-addressed version of a protocol (structured IR), so authorization binds to an exact body.

dataModel.name: validation_results
dataModel.note: Output of the deterministic validator for one protocol version.

dataModel.name: authorizations
dataModel.note: An authorization object — what was authorized, by whom, scoped, signed, and expirable.

fields.name: state
fields.type: enum(active,consumed,expired,revoked) — an authorized object's own lifecycle, distinct from any run it spawns

dataModel.name: instrument_reservations
dataModel.note: A held slot on a physical instrument / reagent set for an authorized run.

dataModel.name: run_attempts
dataModel.note: One physical execution attempt with an idempotent lease — guards exactly-once actuation across retries.

fields.name: conditions
fields.type: jsonb null (environmental conditions captured at run time: temp, humidity, etc.)

fields.name: instrument_calibration_ref
fields.type: varchar null (instrument version + calibration snapshot at run time)

dataModel.name: run_artifacts
dataModel.note: Raw and derived output artifacts produced by a run, each an object-storage reference with a checksum so the exact bytes can be re-fetched and verified.

fields.name: derived_from
fields.type: uuid FK null (lineage: which raw/derived artifact this came from)

dataModel.name: findings
dataModel.note: A derived scientific result that aggregates claims and data.

fields.name: analysis_code_version
fields.type: varchar (pipeline version that turned raw artifacts into the finding)

dataModel.name: claim_provenance
dataModel.note: Maps each claim in a finding to the exact citation span and corpus snapshot it relied on, so it survives corpus updates and retractions. Written at claim time, not extracted post-hoc.

fields.name: corpus_snapshot
fields.type: varchar (corpus version the span was read from)

fields.name: retracted
fields.type: bool (flagged if the source is later retracted)

dataModel.name: datum_provenance
dataModel.note: Maps each datum in a finding to the run attempt, artifact, and protocol version that produced it (reproducibility of data).

fields.name: artifact_id
fields.type: uuid FK (the run_artifacts row this datum was read from)

patterns.name: Workflow vs agent (deterministic pipeline vs probabilistic loop)
patterns.what: An authored DAG (retrieve -> hypothesize -> design -> validate -> authorize -> run), not an autonomous loop driving the lab.
patterns.whenToUse: Where an action drives physical instruments.

patterns.name: Human-in-the-loop approval (gate)
patterns.what: A human authorizes any physical experiment; the agent never actuates the lab on its own.

patterns.name: Bounded autonomy (agent + deterministic gate)
patterns.what: The agent reasons and designs freely but a validator and authorization gate fence physical execution.
patterns.whenToUse: Design-and-dry-run autonomy with a wet-lab boundary.

patterns.name: Memory: short-term vs long-term (semantic/episodic/procedural)
patterns.what: Ground hypotheses in primary literature and prior experimental data.
patterns.whenToUse: Every claim must cite a source.

patterns.name: LLM-as-judge / trajectory evaluation
patterns.what: Evaluate claim grounding and reproducibility against replicated results.

patterns.name: Delegation not impersonation (OAuth token exchange, act claim)
patterns.what: Scoped access; a wet-lab authorization is bound to a researcher / biosafety principal.

patterns.name: Prompt-injection defense (CaMeL / Dual-LLM / Plan-then-Execute)
patterns.what: Treat papers and datasets as untrusted; partition from lab-actuation authority via a structured protocol boundary.
patterns.whenToUse: Literature and data are attacker-reachable input.

patterns.name: Durable execution / checkpointing (resume after crash)
patterns.what: Long experiment runs resume exactly-once and never silently re-actuate.

patterns.name: Idempotency key for physical actuation
patterns.what: Every physical run is submitted with an idempotency key so a retry after an ambiguous timeout resolves to the same run, not a second actuation.

steps.title: 1. Naive: An Agent That Runs Experiments Autonomously (the baseline)
description: Start with the autonomous-scientist demo: an agent forms a hypothesis and drives the lab robot to test it, end to end, on its own.

Two distinct dangers appear. Physical: an unsupervised agent actuating instruments can consume reagents, damage equipment, or run something with biosafety/dual-use implications — irreversible in the physical world. Scientific: claims drawn from the model with no provenance can't be reproduced, which is its own kind of failure for research.
The rest of the interview turns this into a grounded, validated, human-authorized, reproducible pipeline with a wet-lab gate.

view.caption: An agent designs and runs experiments autonomously — uncontrolled physical actuation and unreproducible claims.

steps.decisionPrompt: What two different kinds of irreversibility does an autonomous lab agent risk?
patterns: Workflow vs agent (deterministic pipeline vs probabilistic loop)
whyNow: The naive autonomous run makes the wet-lab gate and reproducibility requirements concrete.

recap.after: An agent can hypothesize and run an experiment.
recap.newRisk: Uncontrolled physical actuation (reagents, biosafety) and claims with no provenance or reproducibility.

traps.trap: Letting the agent actuate the lab autonomously because it can.
traps.why: Physical actions are irreversible and can be hazardous; claims without provenance aren't science.
traps.instead: Ground and cite, validate the protocol, require human authorization for any physical run, and track provenance.

steps.title: 2. Ground in Primary Literature + Prior Data
description: The grounding corpus is the science: primary literature and prior experimental data. Hypotheses and protocols must be grounded in cited sources and existing results, not in the model's recollection.

Ingestion captures source metadata, citation spans (the exact passage supporting a claim), versioned corpora, and freshness/retraction/quality signals — so a later claim can name not just a paper but the span and the corpus version it came from.
Every claim carries a citation from the start — that is what makes downstream reproducibility possible. Papers and datasets are treated as untrusted input throughout.

view.caption: Hypotheses and protocols are grounded in cited primary literature and prior experimental data.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why must every claim carry a citation from the moment it's generated?

options.name: Versioned corpus snapshots + retrieval (chosen, hybrid)
options.description: Ingest primary literature and datasets into a versioned, content-addressed corpus with retraction/quality signals; the agent retrieves from a pinned snapshot, and each claim records the citation span and the corpus version it read. New papers re-index incrementally.
pros: A claim pins the exact span and snapshot it relied on, so findings survive corpus updates and retractions.
Quality/retraction signals can demote or flag sources without rewriting history.
Reproducible: re-running against the same snapshot reads the same sources.
cons: Maintaining snapshots and an ingestion/retraction lifecycle is more work than a flat index.
Snapshot pinning can hide newer evidence unless freshness is surfaced.

options.name: Retrieval-only RAG over a live index
options.description: The agent retrieves from a single always-current index; claims cite a paper id but not a span or version.
pros: Simplest to build; always reflects the newest literature.
cons: Not reproducible — the index moves under you, so a re-run can read different sources.
Paper-level citations can't pin the passage relied on or detect later retractions.

options.name: Frozen curated corpus only
options.description: Ground exclusively in a hand-curated, infrequently-updated corpus.
pros: Highest-quality, fully controlled sources; trivially reproducible.
cons: Stale fast in a fast-moving field; misses recent primary literature.
Curation throughput becomes the bottleneck on what can be grounded.

concepts.definition: Generating each hypothesis/claim with a citation to primary literature or prior data.
concepts.whyItMatters: Ungrounded claims can't be verified or reproduced; provenance has to start at generation.
concepts.example: A proposed mechanism cites the paper and the dataset that motivated it.

concepts.definition: Recording the exact source passage and corpus version that supports each claim, not just a paper-level reference.
concepts.whyItMatters: Reproducibility needs to survive corpus updates and retractions; a span + version pins what was actually relied on.
concepts.example: A claim links to paragraph 3 of paper X in corpus snapshot 2026-06, flagged if X is later retracted.

whyNow: Grounded, cited claims are the basis for the provenance and reproducibility built later.

recap.after: Hypotheses are grounded in cited literature and data.
recap.newRisk: A grounded protocol can still be unsafe or malformed to execute physically.

steps.title: 3. Deterministic Protocol Validation
description: Before anything physical happens, a deterministic validator checks the proposed protocol: it is well-formed, within resource limits, and clears dual-use / biosafety rules. These checks are hard rules the model cannot override.

The validator consumes a structured protocol IR (typed steps, reagents, quantities, instruments), not the model's free-text prose — so the rules evaluate machine-checkable fields, and an injected instruction in a paper can't smuggle past prose parsing.
The LLM is confined to bounded nodes (literature review, hypothesis, protocol drafting); the validation that decides whether a protocol may even be authorized is deterministic and auditable.

view.caption: A deterministic validator checks protocol well-formedness, resource limits, and dual-use/biosafety rules before authorization.

options.name: Deterministic rule engine over a structured IR (chosen, hybrid)
options.description: The agent emits a structured protocol IR; a rule engine evaluates well-formedness, resource limits, and dual-use/biosafety rules. Risk-tiering routes elevated cases to a biosafety officer. Model output is never the safety decision.
pros: Auditable, reproducible, and un-overridable by the model or an injected paper.
Rules are versioned and fail closed when policy evaluation is unavailable.
Risk-tiering keeps low-risk throughput high while escalating the dangerous tail.
cons: Requires maintaining a structured IR and a rule corpus.
Rules can lag novel hazards until updated.

view.caption: Chosen: structured IR + deterministic rules + risk-tiering; the model never makes the safety call.

options.name: Model-as-judge safety review
options.description: A second LLM reviews the protocol prose and decides whether it is safe to authorize.
pros: Cheap to build; flexible across novel cases.
cons: Probabilistic — can be wrong or prompt-injected via the very paper text it reviews.
Not auditable or reproducible; unacceptable as the sole gate for irreversible physical actions.

options.name: Pure deterministic rule engine (no tiering)
options.description: Hard allow/deny rules over the IR with no risk-tier escalation path.
pros: Simple and fully deterministic.
cons: Either too strict (blocks legitimate elevated work) or too loose; no path to route ambiguous cases to a biosafety officer.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why is model-only safety review unacceptable, and which checks must be deterministic and un-overridable?
patterns: Workflow vs agent (deterministic pipeline vs probabilistic loop)

concepts.definition: Rule-based checks (well-formedness, resource limits, biosafety/dual-use) applied to a protocol independently of the model.
concepts.whyItMatters: Safety-critical gating of physical actions can't ride on a probabilistic model that can be wrong or injected.
concepts.example: A protocol exceeding a reagent or containment limit is blocked before it ever reaches authorization.

concepts.definition: A typed, machine-checkable representation of a protocol (steps, reagents, quantities, instruments) the model must emit, separate from its prose.
concepts.whyItMatters: Deterministic rules can only be enforced over structured fields; validating prose invites injection and ambiguity.
concepts.example: The validator reads `reagent: BSL-3 agent, volume: 50mL` from the IR, not from a sentence in the draft.

concepts.definition: Validation rules carry a version; if the policy service is unavailable, the protocol is blocked rather than allowed.
concepts.whyItMatters: An outage must never become an open door to physical actuation.
concepts.example: Policy service down -> validation returns blocked, run never schedules.

whyNow: Deterministic validation is the safety floor beneath the human authorization gate.

traps.trap: Validating the model's free-text protocol prose.
traps.why: Prose hides ambiguity and lets an injected instruction in a paper steer the protocol; rules can't be enforced reliably over text.
traps.instead: Require a structured IR and run deterministic rules over its typed fields; reject IR that doesn't parse.

traps.trap: Failing open when the policy service is unavailable.
traps.why: An outage shouldn't authorize hazardous physical runs.
traps.instead: Fail closed: block validation and record policy-service-unavailable.

recap.before: A protocol could go straight from the model to the lab.
recap.after: A structured protocol is deterministically validated, risk-tiered, and hazardous ones blocked.
recap.newRisk: A valid protocol still must not run physically without a human authorizing it.

steps.title: 4. The Gate: Human Authorization for Any Physical Run
description: This is the crux and the new gate for the series: a wet-lab boundary. A validated protocol is queued for authorization, and any action that consumes reagents, drives a lab robot, or has biosafety implications requires explicit human sign-off — a researcher, plus a biosafety officer when the protocol warrants it. Identity binds the authorization to that principal.
Tiering matters: dry-run analysis and simulation are free; only wet-lab execution needs authorization. The agent designs and analyzes autonomously but never actuates the physical world on its own.

view.caption: Any physical action requires human authorization, producing a scoped authorization object the execution controller consumes; dry-runs are free.

options.name: Durable approval workflow (chosen)
options.description: Authorization is a durable workflow: the validated protocol version is queued, a researcher (plus biosafety officer when required) signs, producing a scoped, expirable authorization object. Approval is decoupled from execution scheduling and survives long waits, crashes, and operator handoffs.
pros: Handles long-running, asynchronous human decisions without holding a request open.
Authorization is an auditable object (signer chain, scope, expiry, revocation), not a flipped flag.
Cleanly separates 'approved' from 'safely executed exactly once'.
cons: Needs a durable workflow/state store and a callback path back to the requester.

view.caption: Chosen: durable approval produces an authorization object the execution controller turns into an idempotent run.

options.name: Synchronous approval inside the API request
options.description: The authorize call blocks until the run completes.
pros: Simplest mental model for short actions.
cons: Wet-lab runs take minutes to days — holding a request is infeasible.
A crash mid-request loses approval state and risks ambiguous re-submission.

options.name: Out-of-band ticket / e-signature only
options.description: Approval happens in an external ticketing/e-sign system; the platform polls or trusts a status flip.
pros: Reuses existing compliance tooling.
cons: Authorization scope isn't bound to the exact protocol version/hash in the platform.
Weak coupling makes exactly-once execution and revocation hard to enforce.

steps.decisionPrompt: Which actions are free for the agent, which require human authorization, and why must approval be a durable object rather than a synchronous call?
patterns: Delegation not impersonation (OAuth token exchange, act claim)

concepts.definition: The line between free dry-run analysis and physical execution that consumes reagents or drives instruments, which requires human authorization.
concepts.whyItMatters: Physical actions are irreversible and potentially hazardous; the boundary is where autonomy must stop.
concepts.example: Simulating a reaction is free; running it on the bench needs a researcher's authorization.

concepts.definition: An explicit record of what was authorized: protocol version/hash, signer chain (researcher + biosafety officer), instrument/reagent scope, expiry, and revocation — not a status flag.
concepts.whyItMatters: An object can be scoped, expired, revoked, and audited; a flag cannot bind approval to an exact protocol body.
concepts.example: Authorization #7 covers version-hash abc on instrument PCR-2, ≤50mL, expiring in 24h.

concepts.definition: Protocol readiness (proposed -> validated|blocked -> awaiting_authorization -> authorized|rejected) is owned by the protocol/authorization records; physical execution (submitted -> running -> succeeded|failed|aborted) is owned by run_attempts. One authorized version can spawn many run attempts (dry-run, wet-lab, replication).
concepts.whyItMatters: Conflating them breaks down the moment a version is run more than once; keeping 'approved' distinct from each 'executed' attempt is what makes the audit trail unambiguous.
concepts.example: Version-hash abc is authorized once but has three run_attempts: a dry-run, a failed wet-lab run, and a successful replication.

whyNow: The wet-lab gate is what makes an autonomous research agent safe to connect to real instruments.

recap.before: The agent could actuate the lab autonomously.
recap.after: Physical runs require human authorization; dry-runs stay free.
recap.newRisk: 'Authorized' is not 'safely executed exactly once' — a retry after a timeout could double-actuate the robot.

steps.title: 4a. Durable Lab Execution (exactly-once physical actuation)
description: Authorization is not execution. Physical runs are long, sometimes ambiguous: a submission can time out after the robot already accepted the job, a run can partially complete, an operator can abort, an instrument can be unavailable, or a callback can go missing. A blind retry could consume reagents twice or submit the same robot job twice — the highest-blast-radius bug in the system.

An execution controller sits between the authorization gate and lab automation as the only component allowed to submit lab jobs. It reserves the instrument/reagents, submits the run with an idempotency key and a short-lived run token, persists a run lease and checkpoints, processes robot callbacks, supports abort/manual stop, and writes an immutable run record before results enter the data store. The robot rejects any job lacking a valid token, so the model can never reach it directly.

view.caption: An execution controller turns an authorization object into an idempotent, leased physical run; dry-runs route to the simulator.

steps.decisionPrompt: After a submission times out, how do you avoid running the same physical experiment twice?

options.name: Durable workflow engine + reservation service (chosen)
options.description: A workflow engine drives each run as a durable, checkpointed saga (reserve -> submit -> await callback -> reconcile -> record), and a reservation service holds the scarce instrument/reagent slot. Idempotency keys, run leases, and reconcile-on-timeout live in the engine's state, so a crash resumes mid-run instead of resubmitting.
pros: Survives crashes and long (minutes-to-days) runs without losing run state.
Reservations decouple scarce-instrument scheduling from submission, and the engine makes exactly-once actuation enforceable.
Abort, reconcile, and revocation are first-class steps with durable history.
cons: Most operational machinery: a workflow engine plus a reservation/lease store to run.
Overkill for a lab with one instrument and only short runs.

options.name: Plain queue + reservation, no durable workflow
options.description: Authorized runs go on a work queue; a worker reserves a slot and submits, retrying on failure.
pros: Simpler than a full workflow engine; familiar queue semantics.
cons: A worker crash mid-run loses checkpoint/lease state, so reconciliation is ad hoc.
At-least-once queue delivery fights exactly-once actuation unless you rebuild idempotency/leases yourself.

options.name: Direct robot API from the pipeline
options.description: The pipeline calls the lab automation API directly once authorization is recorded.
pros: Fewest moving parts; lowest latency for short runs.
cons: No durable run record, lease, or reconcile path — an ambiguous timeout becomes a blind retry that double-actuates.
Couples scarce-instrument scheduling into request handling; can't survive long runs.

concepts.definition: Submitting a physical run with an idempotency key so a retry after an ambiguous timeout resolves to the same run, never a second actuation.
concepts.whyItMatters: Physical actions can't be rolled back; duplicate submission wastes reagents or causes harm.
concepts.example: Retrying run K returns the existing run's status instead of starting a new one on the bench.

concepts.definition: A time-bounded claim on a run that the controller renews; an expired or unknown lease forces reconciliation, not a blind retry.
concepts.whyItMatters: It turns ambiguous 'did it run?' states into a deliberate check rather than a guess.
concepts.example: A missing callback past the lease triggers a status query to the robot before any retry.

whyNow: Durable, idempotent execution is what makes 'authorized' actually safe to turn into a physical action.

flows.name: Submission times out but the robot already accepted the job
flows.note: The controller reconciles instead of resubmitting.

messages.label: K already accepted / running (no new run)

else.label: key K never seen by robot

traps.trap: Retrying a physical submission on timeout without reconciling.
traps.why: The robot may have accepted the first job; a blind retry double-actuates.
traps.instead: Use an idempotency key and query run status before resubmitting; treat 'unknown' as a reconcile state, not a retry.

recap.before: Authorization flowed straight to the robot, with retries risking double actuation.
recap.after: An execution controller makes physical runs idempotent, leased, and reconcilable.
recap.newRisk: Results and claims must be reproducible and fully traceable to be science.

steps.title: 5. Reproducibility & Provenance
description: Make the output science, not just text. A provenance store maps every claim to its source literature and every datum to the run that produced it, so a finding can be traced end to end and an experiment re-run from its recorded protocol and conditions.
The audit record doubles as a complete lab notebook: sources, hypotheses, protocols, validations, authorizations, runs, and results, all append-only.

view.caption: Every claim traces to its source and every datum to its run; the audit record is a complete, append-only lab notebook.

steps.decisionPrompt: What must be recorded so another scientist can reproduce a finding?

options.name: Lineage captured at write time (chosen)
options.description: Provenance is written as the data is produced: each claim records its citation span + corpus snapshot when generated, and each datum records its run, artifact checksum, protocol version, conditions, and analysis-code version when written. Provenance rows are created alongside the artifacts, not derived later.
pros: Complete and trustworthy: nothing has to be reconstructed or guessed afterward.
Survives corpus updates, retractions, and pipeline changes because the exact span/version is pinned at the moment of use.
Makes findings independently re-runnable from recorded inputs.
cons: Every producing component must thread provenance writes through its hot path.
More storage and write coordination than emitting results alone.

options.name: Post-hoc lineage extraction
options.description: Run normally, then reconstruct provenance later by parsing logs, citations, and run records.
pros: Less intrusive to producing components; provenance is a separate batch job.
cons: Lossy: spans, snapshots, and conditions that weren't logged can't be recovered.
Reconstruction can be wrong, and the gaps surface exactly when you need to reproduce a result.

concepts.definition: A traceable chain from each claim to its source and each datum to the run, protocol, and conditions that produced it.
concepts.whyItMatters: Reproducibility is the core scientific requirement; without provenance a result can't be trusted or rebuilt.
concepts.example: A finding links to its source papers and to run #42 with its exact protocol version and parameters.

whyNow: Provenance and reproducibility are what make the platform's output usable as science.

deepDives.title: The lab notebook as audit
points: Append-only record of every source, protocol, authorization, and run.
Each datum carries the run, protocol version, and conditions that produced it.
Raw model traces are stored separately under data governance.

deepDives.title: What a finding must record to be reproducible
points: Protocol version + content hash and the exact run parameters.
Environmental conditions and instrument calibration/version at run time.
Raw result artifact references (object storage) plus derived result lineage.
Analysis code version that turned raw data into the reported finding.
Citation spans and corpus version for every claim that fed the hypothesis.

recap.after: Every claim and datum is traceable and reproducible.
recap.newRisk: Hazardous or dual-use protocols must be screened, and scientific reliability measured.

steps.title: 6. Dual-Use & Biosafety Screening
description: This step is the adversarial hardening pass over the validation introduced in Step 3: some experiments must never run regardless of who asks. The key threat is that papers and datasets are untrusted input — an injected instruction in a paper could try to steer the design agent toward a hazardous protocol.

The defense is structural: untrusted content reaches the agent, but the agent can only emit a structured protocol IR, and deterministic screening evaluates that IR — never the model's prose or the paper's text. Screening separates two outcomes: hard-blocked (a hazard rule matched, un-overridable) versus needs-biosafety (routed to a biosafety officer). Policy rules are versioned and fail closed when the policy service is unavailable.
This is the un-overridable safety floor for the physical world: a protocol the screening flags is stopped no matter how the agent (or an injected instruction in a paper) argues for it.

view.caption: Untrusted paper text flows through the agent into a structured protocol; deterministic screening blocks hazards and routes biosafety cases to an officer.

steps.decisionPrompt: How do you stop an injected instruction in a paper from talking the agent into a hazardous protocol?

concepts.definition: Deterministic checks that block hazardous or dual-use protocols and route biosafety-relevant ones to an officer, independent of the model.
concepts.whyItMatters: The worst physical outcomes must be unreachable even if the model is wrong or manipulated.
concepts.example: A protocol matching a hazard rule is blocked and cannot be authorized through the normal path.

whyNow: Biosafety screening is the hard floor that makes physical autonomy acceptable at all.

flows.name: Injected instruction in a paper is contained
flows.note: Untrusted text can influence the draft but never the safety decision.

messages.label: structured protocol IR (built from untrusted paper)

traps.trap: Letting untrusted paper text reach the safety decision.
traps.why: An injected instruction can argue the model into a hazardous protocol if prose is what gets evaluated.
traps.instead: Screen the structured IR deterministically; keep untrusted content out of the tool-authority path.

recap.before: Avoiding a hazardous protocol depended on the model behaving.
recap.after: Dual-use/biosafety hazards are blocked deterministically (hard-block vs needs-biosafety) and untrusted text is kept out of the safety decision.
recap.newRisk: Scientific reliability across workflows still needs to be measured.

steps.title: 7. Workflows & Scientific-Reliability Evaluation
description: Support the real workflows on this substrate — literature review, hypothesis generation, experimental design, and the design-run-analyze loop — and evaluate what matters for science: claim grounding and reproducibility against replicated results, not just whether an answer sounds plausible.
A confident but unsupported or irreproducible claim is a failure here; the eval weights grounding and replication, and watches the rate of blocked/abstained hazardous proposals.

Concrete metrics worth tracking: citation support rate, unsupported-claim rate, validation block rate, dry-run vs wet-lab conversion, run success/failure/abort rate, replicate pass rate, and time-to-reproduce. Operational signals feed the same observability: unauthorized submission attempts, validator failures, policy-service-unavailable events, run-retry suppression, callback gaps, blocked hazardous proposals, citation coverage, and replication failure rate are what trigger alerts or review.

view.caption: Research workflows run on the pipeline; eval weights claim grounding and reproducibility against replicated results.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why is a plausible-sounding result the wrong thing to optimize for in research?

options.name: Replication-weighted evaluation (chosen)
options.description: Score findings primarily by whether they replicate on re-run and whether every claim is grounded, with citation precision and operational signals as supporting metrics.
pros: Optimizes for the thing science actually requires: results that hold up.
Naturally penalizes confident-but-unsupported and irreproducible outputs.
cons: Replication is expensive and slow; needs sampling and prioritization.
Some findings can't be cheaply replicated and need proxy signals.

options.name: Output plausibility scoring
options.description: An LLM-as-judge rates how plausible/fluent the answer sounds.
pros: Cheap and fast to compute.
cons: Plausibility is not truth; rewards confident fabrication.
Wrong objective for a system that drives physical experiments.

options.name: Citation precision only
options.description: Measure only whether cited sources support the claims.
pros: Directly targets grounding; relatively cheap.
cons: A well-cited claim can still fail to replicate experimentally.
Misses run success/abort and reproducibility outcomes.

concepts.definition: Measuring whether claims are grounded and findings replicate, not just whether outputs are fluent or plausible.
concepts.whyItMatters: Plausibility is not truth; only grounding and replication establish a reliable result.
concepts.example: Track claim-grounding rate and how often a finding replicates on re-run.

whyNow: Reliability-weighted evaluation is what keeps the platform scientifically trustworthy at scale.

recap.after: Grounding and reproducibility are evaluated against replicated results.
recap.newRisk: This completes phase 3 of the series: Foundations plus verticals spanning the full gradient and the real-time, contestability, and physical-experiment axes.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Agentic Research Platform
finalDesign.description: A research goal enters through the API. A deterministic research pipeline has a design agent review primary literature and prior data and draft a hypothesis and a structured protocol with citation spans in bounded LLM nodes. A deterministic validator checks the protocol IR for well-formedness, resource limits, and dual-use/biosafety hazards — blocking hazardous ones, routing biosafety cases to an officer, versioning its rules and failing closed — and the model cannot override it. Validated protocol versions are queued for a durable authorization workflow: any wet-lab action requires a researcher's (and, when warranted, a biosafety officer's) explicit sign-off, producing a scoped, signed, expirable authorization object bound to the exact protocol version/hash, while dry-run analysis routes to the simulator for free. The execution controller is the only component allowed to actuate the lab: it consumes the authorization object, reserves instruments/reagents, mints a run token, submits the run with an idempotency key, holds a run lease, ingests signed robot callbacks (replay-protected by sequence number), and supports abort and reconcile-on-timeout, writing an immutable run record before results enter the data store. Protocol/authorization readiness and physical run state are kept in separate state machines, so one authorized version can spawn many run attempts (dry-run, wet-lab, replication); revocation has well-defined effects before scheduling (run never starts), during reservation (slot released), and after submission (in-flight run aborted). A provenance store maps every claim to its source span and every datum to its run, protocol version, and conditions; the audit record is a complete append-only lab notebook; and evaluation weights claim grounding and reproducibility against replicated results. Grounding, durable execution, identity, and observability are the shared substrate from Agentic Platform Foundations; uniquely, the gate plus execution controller guard a physical, irreversible experiment with exactly-once actuation.

functional.how: Grounded design agent over the literature index and data store.
Bounded LLM design node with citations.
Wet-lab authorization gate bound to a researcher/biosafety principal.
Execution controller submits idempotent, leased runs to lab automation and captures results.

nonFunctional.how: Provenance from claim to source and datum to run.
Wet-lab gate with dry-run vs wet-lab tiering; the execution controller is the only caller allowed to actuate the robot.
Idempotency keys, run leases, and reconcile-on-timeout in the execution controller.
Append-only record of sources, protocols, authorizations, reservations, leases, runs.

technologyChoices.concern: Research API, identity, and delegated authorization
tradeoff: A dedicated gateway and token broker make the wet-lab authorization boundary explicit: the execution controller receives scoped run tokens, not broad researcher credentials.
Managed identity services reduce auth operational work, but self-hosted brokers give tighter control over lab-local principals, signer chains, and offline instrument networks.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A broker that mints scoped, expiring run tokens removes the need to pass user credentials or long-lived API keys into instrument adapters.

tradeoff: Self-hosted serving gives model/version pinning, local data controls, and lower marginal cost for repeated design passes.
Managed model platforms simplify scaling and safety filters, but the deterministic protocol validator must still be the authority for any physical action.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Provider guardrails can reduce prompt and content risk, but they do not replace the structured protocol IR or the fail-closed validator.

technologyChoices.concern: Literature corpus ingestion and hybrid retrieval
tradeoff: Hybrid keyword/vector retrieval fits scientific grounding because exact terms, citation spans, semantic neighbors, corpus snapshots, and retraction signals all matter.
A managed index reduces corpus operations; self-hosting preserves tighter control over snapshotting, custom ranking, and citation-span extraction.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A retrieval system with versioned snapshots and quality signals removes much of the post-hoc work needed to explain which corpus state supported a claim.

technologyChoices.concern: Structured protocol IR and deterministic policy validation
tradeoff: A policy engine keeps resource limits, dual-use rules, and lab constraints versioned, testable, and separate from LLM output.
Schema validators are cheaper and faster for well-formedness, but richer policy engines are better for signer, lab, reagent, and risk-tier constraints.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A tested, fail-closed validation service removes scattered application-level if/else safety checks and makes policy outages block execution by default.

technologyChoices.concern: Durable execution and long-running workflow state
tradeoff: Temporal/Cadence-style engines fit multi-day physical runs because leases, timers, callbacks, retries, and replay are core concepts.
Managed step engines reduce cluster operations but may constrain history size, maximum duration, and how much custom reconciliation logic can live in the workflow.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A real workflow engine removes most hand-rolled retry tables, lease renewals, and reconcile-on-timeout loops from the execution controller.

technologyChoices.concern: Instrument reservation and exactly-once actuation state
tradeoff: Relational constraints make reservations, authorization state, idempotency keys, and run attempts easy to audit together.
A low-latency KV store is attractive for leases and idempotency windows, but durable reservation ownership should still be recoverable after failover.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Conditional writes plus unique idempotency keys remove the need for best-effort duplicate detection after an ambiguous robot submission timeout.

technologyChoices.concern: Lab automation adapters and instrument command boundary
tradeoff: Open lab and industrial protocols give a uniform signed command/callback contract across instruments.
Vendor SDKs expose deeper device capability but fragment idempotency, abort, calibration, and replay-protection semantics.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A narrow adapter boundary prevents the hypothesis agent, notebook UI, or generic workflow steps from ever submitting physical lab commands directly.

technologyChoices.concern: Raw result artifacts and immutable retention
tradeoff: Object storage with checksums, lifecycle tiers, and immutability fits 10MB-50GB run artifacts retained for years.
Self-hosted object stores can satisfy lab-local data residency; managed stores usually give stronger lifecycle, replication, and WORM controls with less operations work.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Object-lock or equivalent WORM storage removes the need to build a separate immutability layer for raw experiment artifacts.

technologyChoices.concern: Provenance graph and scientific metadata catalog
tradeoff: Relational provenance is simpler for protocol, run, artifact, and claim tables; graph stores make cross-run lineage, replicated findings, and citation neighborhoods easier to traverse.
A data catalog helps govern datasets and schemas, but claim-level citation spans still need application-owned records.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Capturing claim and datum lineage at write time removes fragile post-hoc provenance reconstruction from raw notebooks and result files.

technologyChoices.concern: Append-only audit log / lab notebook stream
tradeoff: A stream decouples audit writes from run execution and lets evaluators replay lab-notebook events.
A tamper-evident ledger improves audit guarantees, but a plain event log still needs a long-retention sink and operational controls to prevent mutation.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A central append-only audit channel removes per-service bespoke log exports for protocol, authorization, lease, callback, and result events.

tradeoff: Infrastructure observability explains latency, leases, callback gaps, and policy failures; scientific evaluation explains claim grounding, replication, and reproducibility.
Managed observability reduces telemetry operations, but evaluation metrics still need joins over provenance, run attempts, artifacts, and audit events.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A unified trace and eval pipeline removes one-off scripts that separately inspect workflow history, robot callbacks, and experiment outcomes.

say: Frame the new gate: a physical wet-lab boundary, plus reproducibility as first-class.
It builds on Agentic Platform Foundations.
Draw the autonomous lab agent and name the two irreversibilities: physical and scientific.
Ground and cite claims, then deterministic protocol validation with biosafety rules.
Durable human authorization for any physical run (dry-run vs wet-lab) producing a scoped authorization object.
Then an execution controller for exactly-once actuation: reservations, idempotency keys, leases, callbacks.
Provenance from claim-span to run, and what a finding must record to be reproducible.
Dual-use/biosafety screening and reproducibility-weighted eval, not plausibility.

expectations: Agent reviews literature and proposes a protocol.
Knows a human authorizes physical runs.
Cited grounding and a deterministic protocol validator.
Wet-lab authorization gate; provenance for findings.
Tiers dry-run vs wet-lab and puts biosafety screening in an un-overridable layer over a structured protocol IR.
Designs durable execution for exactly-once physical actuation (idempotency keys, leases, reconcile-on-timeout).
Evaluates grounding and replication, not plausibility; builds on the Foundations substrate.

followUps: How do you tier actions into free dry-runs vs gated wet-lab execution?
After a robot submission times out, how do you guarantee the experiment runs exactly once?
Why must the validator consume a structured protocol IR rather than the model's prose?
What must be recorded for a finding to be independently reproducible?
How do you keep dual-use/biosafety screening robust against injected instructions in a paper?
How does the physical-experiment gate compare to the healthcare and finance gates in the series?

links.group: Autonomous Scientific Agents and Self-Driving Labs
links.groupDescription: Research systems that connect language-model planning to scientific tools, experiments, and robotic labs.
links.title: Autonomous chemical research with large language models
links.why: Concrete Coscientist case study for the baseline: an LLM-driven system uses web search, documentation search, code execution, and lab automation, showing both the promise and the risk of autonomous physical experiments.

links.group: Autonomous Scientific Agents and Self-Driving Labs
links.groupDescription: Research systems that connect language-model planning to scientific tools, experiments, and robotic labs.
links.title: ChemCrow: Augmenting large-language models with chemistry tools
links.why: Shows a chemistry agent augmented with domain tools, motivating why the platform must treat tool use as a governed capability rather than free-form model autonomy.

links.group: Autonomous Scientific Agents and Self-Driving Labs
links.groupDescription: Research systems that connect language-model planning to scientific tools, experiments, and robotic labs.
links.title: An autonomous laboratory for the accelerated synthesis of inorganic materials
links.why: Industrial-strength self-driving-lab reference for experiment planning, robotic synthesis, characterization, and iterative materials discovery.

links.group: Autonomous Scientific Agents and Self-Driving Labs
links.groupDescription: Research systems that connect language-model planning to scientific tools, experiments, and robotic labs.
links.title: A mobile robotic chemist
links.why: Grounds the lab-execution problem in real autonomous hardware, where physical movement, instrument state, and experiment results must be coordinated safely.

links.group: Autonomous Scientific Agents and Self-Driving Labs
links.groupDescription: Research systems that connect language-model planning to scientific tools, experiments, and robotic labs.
links.title: The AI Scientist: Towards Fully Automated Open-Ended Scientific Discovery
links.why: End-to-end agentic research workflow for idea generation, experiment execution, paper writing, and automated review, useful when comparing dry-run autonomy with wet-lab gates.

links.groupDescription: Primary references for citation-backed scientific retrieval, corpus APIs, and literature-grounded claim generation.
links.title: Language agents achieve superhuman synthesis of scientific knowledge
links.why: PaperQA2 and LitQA2 directly inform cited literature synthesis, contradiction detection, and benchmarks for grounded scientific claims.

links.groupDescription: Primary references for citation-backed scientific retrieval, corpus APIs, and literature-grounded claim generation.
links.title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks
links.why: Foundational retrieval-augmented generation reference for external, updateable memory and provenance-aware answers instead of relying on model parameters alone.

links.groupDescription: Primary references for citation-backed scientific retrieval, corpus APIs, and literature-grounded claim generation.
links.title: S2ORC: The Semantic Scholar Open Research Corpus
links.why: Describes a large machine-readable scholarly corpus with metadata, abstracts, references, and structured full text, useful for building a literature index with citation spans.

links.groupDescription: Primary references for citation-backed scientific retrieval, corpus APIs, and literature-grounded claim generation.
links.title: OpenAlex Developers: Overview
links.why: Open research graph and API documentation for works, authors, institutions, topics, funders, and snapshots, useful for corpus freshness and citation-graph enrichment.

links.groupDescription: Primary references for citation-backed scientific retrieval, corpus APIs, and literature-grounded claim generation.
links.title: The E-utilities In-Depth: Parameters, Syntax and More
links.why: Official NCBI API reference for searching and retrieving biomedical records, a practical source for PubMed-backed grounding and rate-limit-aware ingestion.

links.groupDescription: Structured protocol and instrument-interface references for turning natural-language plans into machine-checkable lab actions.
links.title: autoprotocol/autoprotocol-python
links.why: Shows a JSON-serializable protocol representation for life-science experiments, matching the platform's need for a structured protocol IR instead of prose.

links.groupDescription: Structured protocol and instrument-interface references for turning natural-language plans into machine-checkable lab actions.
links.title: Python Protocol API Documentation
links.why: Concrete robot-control API for labware, pipettes, modules, and command sequences, useful for understanding the execution boundary behind the wet-lab gate.

links.groupDescription: Structured protocol and instrument-interface references for turning natural-language plans into machine-checkable lab actions.
links.title: SiLA Rapid Integration
links.why: Official lab-automation standards reference for connecting instruments, informatics systems, and lab data without custom point-to-point integrations.

links.group: Durable Execution and Physical Actuation Safety
links.groupDescription: Workflow and idempotency references for long-running, retry-prone physical lab runs.
links.title: Temporal Workflow
links.why: Explains event-history replay, deterministic workflow code, activity recording, and resume-after-failure semantics that map to lab run leases and checkpoints.

links.group: Durable Execution and Physical Actuation Safety
links.groupDescription: Workflow and idempotency references for long-running, retry-prone physical lab runs.
links.title: Making retries safe with idempotent APIs
links.why: Strong distributed-systems reference for caller-provided request IDs, duplicate suppression, reconciliation, and why blind retries are dangerous for side-effectful operations.

links.groupDescription: Standards for tracing claims, data, people, activities, and workflows into reproducible research objects.
links.title: PROV-Overview: An Overview of the PROV Family of Documents
links.why: Defines a provenance vocabulary for entities, activities, people, derivations, and responsibility — a direct fit for claim-to-run traceability.

links.groupDescription: Standards for tracing claims, data, people, activities, and workflows into reproducible research objects.
links.title: RO-Crate Metadata Specification 1.3
links.why: A practical JSON-LD packaging standard for research objects, datasets, workflows, scripts, and provenance metadata.

links.groupDescription: Safety and security references for blocking hazardous physical protocols and keeping untrusted documents away from privileged tool authority.
links.title: Laboratory biosafety manual, 4th edition
links.why: Global biosafety reference for risk assessment and laboratory safety controls, grounding the biosafety-officer route and fail-closed screening path.

links.groupDescription: Safety and security references for blocking hazardous physical protocols and keeping untrusted documents away from privileged tool authority.
links.title: LLM01:2025 Prompt Injection
links.why: Threat model and mitigations for direct and indirect prompt injection from papers, datasets, and retrieved documents before they reach privileged tools.

links.groupDescription: Safety and security references for blocking hazardous physical protocols and keeping untrusted documents away from privileged tool authority.
links.title: Defeating Prompt Injections by Design
links.why: System-level defense that separates control flow from untrusted data, matching the design's structured IR and deterministic screening boundary.

links.groupDescription: Benchmarks and evaluation methods for measuring practical scientific-research capability, not just fluent answers.
links.title: LAB-Bench: Measuring Capabilities of Language Models for Biology Research
links.why: Evaluates practical biology-research tasks such as literature search, protocol planning, database navigation, and sequence reasoning — close to the platform's reliability metrics.
